lessons.title: Sinusoids and the Phasor Representation
lessons.content:
A sinusoidal signal is fully described by three numbers: amplitude $V_m$, angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$, and phase $\phi$:

$$ v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi). $$

In **linear, single-frequency steady state**, every voltage and current in a circuit is a sinusoid at that same $\omega$ — only amplitude and phase change. So why drag $\omega t$ through every equation? We don't. A **phasor** strips out the common $\omega t$ and keeps just the complex amplitude:

$$ \mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} = V_m\angle\phi. $$

The link is Euler's identity, $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$. We write $v(t) = \mathrm{Re}\{\mathbf{V} e^{j\omega t}\}$, picturing the phasor as a vector rotating counter-clockwise at $\omega$; the real (horizontal) projection is the instantaneous value. Because all phasors in the circuit rotate together, the rotation cancels and we can do plain **complex algebra** instead of differential equations.

The payoff: differentiation $\tfrac{d}{dt}$ becomes multiplication by $j\omega$, and integration becomes division by $j\omega$. If you spent time on a radar bench, this is exactly the I/Q ($\mathrm{Re}$/$\mathrm{Im}$) decomposition you already know — a phasor is just an I/Q pair frozen at one frequency.

Key conversions you must be fluent in: time $\to$ phasor ($V_m\cos(\omega t+\phi) \Rightarrow V_m\angle\phi$) and back. Note a $\sin$ source is $\cos$ shifted by $-90^\circ$. Phasors add like vectors, which is why KVL and KCL still hold in the phasor domain.

items.front: What three parameters completely describe a sinusoid in the form $v(t)=V_m\cos(\omega t+\phi)$?
items.back: Amplitude $V_m$, angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ (rad/s), and phase $\phi$ (relative to the chosen reference). In single-frequency steady state, $\omega$ is common to all signals, so only $V_m$ and $\phi$ vary node-to-node.
items.hint: How big, how fast, where it starts.

items.front: Define the phasor of $v(t)=V_m\cos(\omega t+\phi)$.
items.back: $\mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} = V_m\angle\phi$. It is the complex amplitude with the common $e^{j\omega t}$ factored out: $v(t)=\mathrm{Re}\{\mathbf{V}e^{j\omega t}\}$.
items.hint: Strip the rotation, keep amplitude and phase.

items.front: Which identity connects the phasor to the time-domain sinusoid?
items.back: Euler's identity $e^{j\theta}=\cos\theta + j\sin\theta$. It lets us treat $V_m\cos(\omega t+\phi)$ as the real part of the rotating complex exponential $\mathbf{V}e^{j\omega t}$.
items.hint: Named after a Swiss mathematician; involves a complex exponential.

items.front: In the phasor domain, what operations replace $\frac{d}{dt}$ and $\int dt$?
items.back: Differentiation becomes multiplication by $j\omega$; integration becomes division by $j\omega$. This is what turns differential equations into algebra.
items.hint: Both involve the factor $j\omega$.

items.front: Why are phasors only valid for linear circuits in single-frequency sinusoidal steady state?
items.back: The method assumes every signal is a sinusoid at the same $\omega$, so the $e^{j\omega t}$ factor cancels everywhere. Nonlinear elements create new frequencies (harmonics), and transients add non-sinusoidal components, both of which break that assumption.
items.hint: What happens to the frequency content in a nonlinear or transient situation?

items.explanation: For a cosine reference, the phasor is amplitude-angle-phase: $V_m\angle\phi = 10\angle -30^\circ$. The amplitude (10) and phase ($-30^\circ$) carry over directly; $\omega t$ is dropped.

items.prompt: Using a cosine reference, the phasor for $i(t) = 4\sin(\omega t)$ is:
items.explanation: $\sin(\omega t) = \cos(\omega t - 90^\circ)$, so the phasor is $4\angle -90^\circ$. Converting sine to cosine always subtracts $90^\circ$ from the phase.

items.prompt: Geometrically, what does $\mathbf{V}e^{j\omega t}$ represent, and how is the instantaneous value recovered?
choices: A vector fixed in place; take its magnitude
A vector rotating CCW at $\omega$; take its real (horizontal) projection
A vector rotating CW at $2\omega$; take its imaginary part
A static point on the real axis; integrate it over time
items.explanation: $\mathbf{V}e^{j\omega t}$ is a vector rotating counter-clockwise at $\omega$. The instantaneous value is its real-axis projection, $v(t)=\mathrm{Re}\{\mathbf{V}e^{j\omega t}\}$ — exactly the I/Q picture from radar.

items.prompt: Why do KVL and KCL still apply directly to phasors?
choices: Because the conservation laws are linear, and phasors add like vectors (superposition of complex amplitudes)
Because energy is not conserved in AC circuits
items.explanation: KVL and KCL are linear sum laws. Since each sinusoid maps to a complex amplitude and complex addition is linear, the laws carry over unchanged to phasor sums.

lessons.title: RMS Values, Power Factor, and the Power Triangle
lessons.content:
AC quantities swing positive and negative, so a single useful number is the **RMS** (root-mean-square) value: the DC value that would dissipate the same average power in a resistor. For a sinusoid,

$$ V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,V_m. $$

This is why "120 V" mains has a peak of about 170 V. When voltage and current are out of phase by angle $\phi$, **average (real) power** is

$$ P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi \quad [\text{watts}], $$

where $\cos\phi$ is the **power factor (PF)**. Real power does actual work and heats things up. The energy that sloshes in and out of reactive elements each cycle is **reactive power**:

$$ Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi \quad [\text{VAR}]. $$

The magnitude the wiring and source actually carry is **apparent power**:

$$ S = V_{rms} I_{rms} \quad [\text{VA}]. $$

These three form the **power triangle**, a right triangle with $P$ along the base, $Q$ vertical, and $S$ as the hypotenuse:

$$ S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}, \qquad \text{PF} = \cos\phi = \frac{P}{S}. $$

A purely resistive load has $\phi = 0$, $\mathrm{PF}=1$, and all apparent power is real. A purely reactive load has $\phi = \pm 90^\circ$, $\mathrm{PF}=0$, and delivers zero average power while still loading the wiring. **Lagging** PF (current lags voltage) means an inductive load; **leading** PF means capacitive. Low PF wastes capacity and is why utilities penalize it and engineers add power-factor-correction capacitors. Crucially, average power flows only through the in-phase ($\cos\phi$) component of current.

items.front: Define RMS value in terms of equivalent power.
items.back: The RMS value of an AC waveform is the DC value that would deliver the same average power to a resistor. It is the root of the mean of the square of the waveform.
items.hint: Same heating effect as what kind of source?

items.front: What is the RMS value of a sinusoid with peak amplitude $V_m$?
items.back: $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,V_m$. (This $\sqrt{2}$ factor holds only for sinusoids; other waveshapes have different crest factors.)
items.hint: Divide the peak by $\sqrt{2}$.

items.front: Write the formula for average (real) power in an AC circuit and name the power factor.
items.back: $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ watts. The term $\cos\phi$ (cosine of the voltage-current phase difference) is the power factor.
items.hint: Real power uses the cosine of the phase angle.

items.front: Identify the three sides of the power triangle and their units.
items.back: Real power $P$ (watts, W) along the base, reactive power $Q = V_{rms}I_{rms}\sin\phi$ (volt-amperes reactive, VAR) vertical, and apparent power $S = V_{rms}I_{rms}$ (volt-amperes, VA) as the hypotenuse. $S=\sqrt{P^2+Q^2}$.
items.hint: W, VAR, VA.

items.front: What is the difference between a lagging and a leading power factor?
items.back: Lagging PF: current lags voltage (net inductive load, $\phi>0$). Leading PF: current leads voltage (net capacitive load, $\phi<0$). PF magnitude is $|\cos\phi|$ in both cases.
items.hint: Think inductive vs capacitive.

items.prompt: A load draws 5 A RMS at 120 V RMS with a power factor of 0.8 lagging. What is the real power $P$?
items.explanation: $P = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\phi = 120 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 480$ W. Apparent power $S = 600$ VA, so the rest is reactive and does no work.

items.prompt: For the same load ($S = 600$ VA, $P = 480$ W), what is the reactive power $Q$?
A purely reactive load ($\phi = 90^\circ$) is connected to an AC source. How much average real power does it consume?
items.explanation: Real power is $P = VI\cos\phi$. At $\phi = 90^\circ$, $\cos\phi = 0$, so $P = 0$. Energy only sloshes back and forth (all reactive $Q$); none is dissipated.

items.prompt: Why do utilities and engineers care about correcting a low power factor?
choices: Low PF increases the real power delivered for free
Low PF means high apparent/reactive current that loads wiring and sources without doing useful work
Low PF lowers the RMS voltage at the load
Low PF changes the line frequency
items.explanation: At low PF the apparent power (and thus current) is large relative to the real power consumed. That extra current heats conductors and uses source/transformer capacity without doing work, so PF-correction capacitors are added to bring $\cos\phi$ near 1.

lessons.title: Frequency Response and the Transfer Function H(jw)
lessons.content:
Once a circuit is in phasor form, the ratio of an output phasor to an input phasor is the **transfer function**:

$$ H(j\omega) = \frac{\mathbf{V}_{out}(j\omega)}{\mathbf{V}_{in}(j\omega)}. $$

It is a complex number at each frequency, carrying two pieces of information: the **magnitude** $|H(j\omega)|$ is the gain (output/input amplitude ratio), and the **phase** $\angle H(j\omega)$ is the phase shift the circuit imposes. Together, magnitude and phase versus $\omega$ are the **frequency response**.

Take the canonical RC low-pass filter. Using the impedance divider with $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$:

$$ H(j\omega) = \frac{1/(j\omega C)}{R + 1/(j\omega C)} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega R C}. $$

Define the **corner (cutoff) frequency** $\omega_c = 1/(RC)$. Then

$$ |H| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/\omega_c)^2}}, \qquad \angle H = -\arctan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right). $$

At $\omega = \omega_c$: $|H| = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ (the **$-3$ dB point**, since $20\log_{10}(0.707)\approx-3$ dB) and the phase is $-45^\circ$. Far below $\omega_c$ the filter passes signals nearly unchanged ($|H|\to 1$, phase $\to 0$); far above, it attenuates ($|H|\to 0$, phase $\to -90^\circ$).

The roots of the numerator are **zeros**; the roots of the denominator are **poles**. Here there is a single pole at $\omega_c$. This transfer-function viewpoint is the seam between analog circuits and DSP/control: poles and zeros placed in the complex plane determine filtering, bandwidth, and ultimately feedback stability.

items.front: Define the transfer function $H(j\omega)$ of a linear circuit.
items.back: $H(j\omega) = \mathbf{V}_{out}(j\omega)/\mathbf{V}_{in}(j\omega)$, the complex ratio of output phasor to input phasor as a function of frequency. Its magnitude is the gain; its angle is the phase shift.
items.hint: Output phasor over input phasor.

items.front: What two pieces of information does $H(j\omega)$ encode at each frequency?
items.back: The magnitude $|H(j\omega)|$ = amplitude gain (output/input), and the phase $\angle H(j\omega)$ = phase shift imposed on the signal. Plotted vs $\omega$, these are the frequency response.
items.hint: Gain and... phase.

items.front: Write $H(j\omega)$ for an RC low-pass filter and its corner frequency.
items.back: $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega R C}$, with corner frequency $\omega_c = 1/(RC)$ (equivalently $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$).
items.hint: The capacitor's impedance is in the denominator divider.

items.front: What are poles and zeros of a transfer function?
items.back: Zeros are the values where the numerator goes to zero (gain $\to 0$); poles are where the denominator goes to zero (gain $\to\infty$ in the idealized expression). They set the shape of the magnitude and phase response.
items.hint: Numerator roots vs denominator roots.

items.front: For the one-pole RC low-pass, what are $|H|$ and $\angle H$ exactly at $\omega = \omega_c$?
items.back: $|H| = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ (the $-3$ dB point) and $\angle H = -45^\circ$.
items.hint: Set $\omega/\omega_c = 1$ in the magnitude and arctan expressions.

items.prompt: An RC low-pass filter has $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. What is its corner frequency $f_c$?
For the RC low-pass $H(j\omega) = 1/(1+j\omega RC)$, what is the phase shift as $\omega \to \infty$?
items.explanation: $\angle H = -\arctan(\omega/\omega_c)$. As $\omega\to\infty$, the arctangent approaches $90^\circ$, so the phase approaches $-90^\circ$ — the maximum lag a single real pole can produce.

items.prompt: The magnitude $|H| = 1/\sqrt{2}$ corresponds to how many decibels?
items.explanation: $20\log_{10}(1/\sqrt{2}) = 20\log_{10}(0.707) \approx -3.01$ dB. This is why the corner frequency is called the $-3$ dB point; it is also the half-power point ($|H|^2 = 1/2$).

items.prompt: Why is the transfer-function / pole-zero viewpoint considered the bridge to DSP and control theory?
choices: Because it only works for resistive circuits
Because pole/zero locations in the complex plane determine filtering, bandwidth, and stability across all those domains
Because it eliminates the need for frequency information
Because it converts AC into DC
items.explanation: Filters, digital signal processing, and feedback/control all describe systems by where poles and zeros sit in the complex plane. That common language is why analog frequency response generalizes directly to DSP (z-domain) and control (s-domain).

lessons.title: Bode Plots: Sketching Frequency Response by Hand
lessons.content:
A **Bode plot** displays a transfer function as two graphs versus a **logarithmic** frequency axis: magnitude in **decibels** ($|H|_{dB} = 20\log_{10}|H|$) and phase in degrees. Logarithms turn the products of factors into sums of straight-line **asymptotes**, so a complicated $H(j\omega)$ can be sketched piece by piece.

The core building block is a single real pole, $H = 1/(1 + j\omega/\omega_c)$:

- **Below** $\omega_c$: $|H|_{dB} \approx 0$ dB (flat asymptote).
- **Above** $\omega_c$: magnitude rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade (a tenfold frequency increase drops gain by 20 dB; equivalently $-6$ dB/octave).
- At $\omega_c$ the true curve is $-3$ dB below the asymptote corner.

Phase for that pole sweeps from $0^\circ$ to $-90^\circ$, passing $-45^\circ$ at $\omega_c$, with the transition spanning roughly a decade below to a decade above ($0.1\omega_c$ to $10\omega_c$). A **zero** does the mirror image: $+20$ dB/decade and $+90^\circ$.

To sketch any rational $H(j\omega)$:

1. Factor into poles, zeros, and a DC gain term; convert the gain to dB.
2. Mark each corner frequency on the log axis.
3. Start with the low-frequency asymptote, then add $\pm 20$ dB/decade at each pole/zero corner.
4. Sketch phase the same way using the per-corner $\pm 90^\circ$ contributions.

The attached figure shows the one-pole low-pass magnitude: the flat asymptote, the $-20$ dB/decade slope past $\omega_c$, and the $-3$ dB actual curve at the corner. Build this skill until you can read poles/zeros off a measured response and sketch a plot in seconds — it is the working language of filters, amplifier bandwidth, and feedback stability (phase margin).

items.front: What are the two axes of a Bode magnitude plot?
items.back: Frequency on a logarithmic horizontal axis (decades), and magnitude in decibels on the vertical axis, where $|H|_{dB} = 20\log_{10}|H|$.
items.hint: One axis is log frequency; the other uses dB.

items.front: How do you convert a voltage-gain magnitude to decibels?
items.back: $|H|_{dB} = 20\log_{10}|H|$. (For power ratios it is $10\log_{10}$; the factor of 20 accounts for power being proportional to voltage squared.)
items.hint: 20 times the base-10 log.

items.front: What is the high-frequency asymptotic slope of a single-pole low-pass magnitude response?
items.back: $-20$ dB/decade (equivalently $-6$ dB/octave): gain falls 20 dB for every tenfold increase in frequency above the corner.
items.hint: Per decade, per single pole.

items.front: Describe the phase contribution of a single real pole across frequency.
items.back: Phase sweeps from $0^\circ$ (well below $\omega_c$) to $-90^\circ$ (well above), passing $-45^\circ$ at $\omega_c$. The transition spans roughly $0.1\omega_c$ to $10\omega_c$ (a decade either side).
items.hint: 0 to -90 degrees, with -45 at the corner.

items.front: How does a zero's Bode contribution differ from a pole's?
items.back: A zero is the mirror image: it adds $+20$ dB/decade to the magnitude slope and contributes up to $+90^\circ$ of phase (passing $+45^\circ$ at its corner), whereas a pole gives $-20$ dB/decade and $-90^\circ$.
items.hint: Same shape, opposite sign.

items.prompt: A one-pole low-pass rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade. By how much is the gain reduced two decades above $\omega_c$ (relative to the passband asymptote)?
items.explanation: At $-20$ dB/decade, two decades give $2 \times (-20) = -40$ dB below the flat passband asymptote.

items.prompt: On a Bode magnitude plot, how far is the true one-pole curve below the asymptote intersection at the corner frequency $\omega_c$?
items.explanation: The two straight-line asymptotes meet at the corner, but the actual curve is $20\log_{10}(1/\sqrt2)\approx -3$ dB below that point — the largest deviation from the asymptotes.

items.prompt: A transfer function has a DC gain of 40 dB, one pole at 100 Hz, and one pole at 10 kHz. What is the magnitude slope between 100 Hz and 10 kHz?
items.explanation: Below 100 Hz the response is flat at 40 dB. The first pole at 100 Hz adds $-20$ dB/decade, so between the two corners the slope is $-20$ dB/decade. Above 10 kHz the second pole makes it $-40$ dB/decade.

items.prompt: Why is fluent Bode sketching emphasized as a downstream skill?
choices: It is only needed for DC bias calculations
It is the working language for filters, amplifier bandwidth, and feedback stability / phase margin
It replaces the need for transfer functions
It applies only to nonlinear circuits
items.explanation: Bode reasoning lets you read gain/bandwidth, design filters, and assess feedback stability via phase margin at a glance — which is why it recurs throughout filters, amplifiers, and control modules.
